serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kali Mirch Ka Murgh Badami Kebab is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 3–4 skewered chicken pieces as served at an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Kali Mirch Ka Murgh Badami Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kali Mirch Ka Murgh Badami Kebab (cr_id G00010796). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100g based on the known fatty acid profile of cream-and-almond-marinated chicken preparations; this specific sub-fraction is not separately reported in the HPB entry. Detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish; those fields are set to -1 per Veritas sentinel convention.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate marinated grilled chicken with nuts
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kali Mirch Ka Murgh Badami Kebab. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of marinated grilled chicken with nuts and minimal carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g). Grilled chicken dishes with nut-based marinades and low net carbohydrate consistently fall in the low-GI range (GI 30–45) per University of Sydney GI Database category anchors. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 5.24 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.75, rounded to 3.1 accounting for the full serving carbohydrate contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1080 mg/100g is strongly contraindicated, and even a 150 g serving delivers 1620 mg sodium, representing 70–81% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low GI (~35) support glycemic stability, but high total fat (26.4 g/100g) and high sodium require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; however, the high fat content (26.4 g/100g, including ~7.5 g saturated fat) may contribute to insulin resistance if consumed in excess, and the high sodium (1080 mg/100g) warrants caution in those with comorbid hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1080 mg/100g) and elevated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) are primary cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol at 85 mg/100g adds additional concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions; the combination of high sodium (1080 mg/100g), saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) poses meaningful cardiovascular risk, and preparation modifications such as reducing salt and substituting cream with low-fat yogurt are strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (almonds, black pepper piperine) partially offset by high sodium and saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The almond and black pepper content provides anti-inflammatory benefit via vitamin E, MUFA, and piperine, but the high sodium (1080 mg/100g) may worsen RA-related tissue swelling; request reduced-salt preparation and yogurt-based marinade in place of heavy cream.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kali Mirch Ka Murgh Badami Kebab is an Indian spiced grilled chicken kebab dish featuring chicken cubes marinated in a blend of black pepper (kali mirch), ground almonds (badam), cinnamon, aromatic herbs, and cream or yogurt, then cooked over a flame or in a tandoor to impart a characteristic smoky flavour. The name translates from Hindi as 'black pepper almond chicken kebab'. The dish is rich, mildly spiced, and nutty in flavour, with a tender, slightly charred exterior and a creamy, fragrant interior. It is commonly served as a starter or main course at Indian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat (of which approximately 7.5 g is saturated), 5.24 g carbohydrate, 2.85 g sugar, and 1080 mg sodium. The high fat content derives primarily from almonds and cream used in the marinade. The sodium level is critically elevated at 1080 mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant preparation modification. The low carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100g) and estimated low glycemic index (~35) make it relatively suitable for glycemic management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. Almonds contribute calcium (118 mg/100g), potassium (387 mg/100g), and anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fats. Black pepper provides piperine, which has documented anti-inflammatory and bioavailability-enhancing properties. This dish is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.

The HPB entry is an approved primary source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat sub-fraction is estimated from the known composition of cream-and-almond-marinated chicken and is the only value not directly reported by HPB.

GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate marinated grilled chicken with nuts per University of Sydney GI Database category anchors; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. This dish is served in Singapore at Indian restaurants and is listed in the HPB Foodo database.
